does: This paper identifies and quantifies a gap between aligned LLM behavior and rational decision-making called rational value risk. The authors formally define this metric as the utility loss arising from a deployed reasoning strategy compared to the optimal response that maximizes expected utility in the steepest direction. They decompose estimation error into three sources: limited candidate responses, finite prompt variations, and imperfect reward verifiers. Through extensive empirical evaluation across multiple model families (Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5, Tulu-3, GPT-4.5/5.5, DeepSeek-V4) and reasoning benchmarks (UltraFeedback, AlpacaEval, GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, MathArena), they demonstrate that rational value risk is pervasive even in well-aligned models, that additional alignment reduces but does not eliminate it, that inference-time reasoning choices significantly impact rationality, and that longer reasoning chains show diminishing returns in improving rational behavior.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature distinguishing between value alignment and actual optimal decision-making in LLMs. It extends prior work on LLM alignment through reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and related post-training methods by questioning whether these approaches achieve true utility maximization or merely apparent alignment. The paper connects to research on reasoning quality in language models, chain-of-thought prompting, and reward modeling, while responding to limitations of existing alignment evaluation frameworks that assume aligned models automatically make rational decisions. It also relates to classical decision theory and rational choice theory, bringing formal decision-theoretic concepts like expected utility maximization to the study of LLM behavior.
enables: This work opens several research directions for improving LLM rationality beyond traditional alignment approaches. It suggests investigating whether modified training objectives that explicitly target rational value maximization rather than reward matching could reduce this risk gap. The decomposition of estimation error invites further work on better verifier design and improved candidate generation strategies at inference time. The observation that longer reasoning chains show diminishing returns motivates research into which reasoning patterns genuinely improve rationality versus which merely increase computation without fundamental improvements. More broadly, this framework could inform the development of more robust alignment techniques that guarantee not just value agreement but actual utility-maximizing behavior, with implications for building trustworthy AI systems where internal reasoning quality matches external alignment objectives.
